title: Cat Swollen Fluid Pocket on Ear Flap: Triage Guide for Aural Hematomas
meta_desc: Noticed a fluid-filled swelling on your cat's ear? It may be an aural hematoma. Learn about symptoms, urgency levels, and why photos help your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: A soft, fluid-filled swelling on your cat's ear is likely an aural hematoma, which requires veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours to manage pain and prevent permanent deformity.

What causes a soft, swollen pocket on my cat's ear flap?

A soft, fluid-filled swelling on a cat's ear flap is most commonly an aural hematoma. This condition occurs when small blood vessels within the ear cartilage rupture, usually due to trauma or self-injury. The blood pools between the skin and the ear cartilage, creating a pillow-like or balloon-like appearance that may feel warm or firm to the touch.

Is a swollen ear flap a medical emergency for my cat?

  • The urgency level for an aural hematoma is Medium.
  • While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is a painful and uncomfortable condition that requires a veterinary visit within 24 to 48 hours.
  • If left untreated, the fluid may eventually be reabsorbed, but the ear is likely to shrivel and develop a permanent deformity known as cauliflower ear, which can obstruct the ear canal and lead to chronic infections.

How does taking a photo of my cat's ear help with veterinary triage?

  • Capturing a clear photo allows the vet to see the initial size and location of the swelling.
  • It is useful for monitoring whether the swelling is growing rapidly over time.
  • A photo helps the vet distinguish between a hematoma and other potential issues like an abscess or an insect sting before you even step into the clinic.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Auricular hematomas are fluid-filled swellings that develop on the concave surface of the pinna in cats. These hematomas can range from small to large. The underlying cause is often unknown, but head shaking or ear scratching due to pruritus is almost always involved. Trauma can result in rapid hematoma development, with blood and serum filling the space between skin and cartilage. Treatment involves surgical drainage of the hematoma. After draining and flushing, mattress sutures can be placed to eliminate the "pocket."

Chapter: Surgery, Dermatology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 524)

faqs.question: What causes aural hematomas in cats?
faqs.answer: They are typically caused by trauma from vigorous head shaking or scratching. This is often triggered by an underlying issue such as ear mites, an ear infection, or skin allergies.

faqs.question: Can I drain the fluid pocket at home?
faqs.answer: No, you should never attempt to drain an ear hematoma at home. Doing so introduces a high risk of infection and the pocket will likely refill immediately. Professional veterinary care is required to address the source of the bleeding.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to treat it?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves draining the fluid, often through a small incision or needle aspiration. In many cases, surgery is required to place sutures that prevent the pocket from refilling, alongside treating the underlying cause of the head shaking.
